planting.note: Set out into baskets after the last frost.

directions: Start seed on the surface of a damp mix indoors, keeping warmth and light for two to three weeks. This trailing selection cascades with light-blue flowers and is a longtime basket standard. Keep the roots continuously moist and clip the stems in midsummer to bring back a fuller display.

planting.note: Plant out after frost into baskets or edges.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors and leave uncovered under bright light until seedlings emerge. This trailing form spills pure white flowers, making a cool, airy skirt for baskets and window boxes. Provide steady moisture and part sun, then shear midseason to keep the white cascade dense.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost into warmed soil.

directions: Press seed onto damp starting mix indoors and keep it lit and moist until it sprouts. This trailing color shows rosy-red flowers, each with a white throat, for a warmer note in mixed baskets. Water often so the cascading stems stay turgid and shear back midsummer to renew flowering.

planting.note: Plant into baskets after frost danger has passed.

directions: Start the fine seed on the surface of moist mix indoors and keep it uncovered and bright until it germinates. This trailing Fountain color arches out with clear blue flowers for hanging displays. Keep the container mix evenly damp and shear the stems midseason to keep the fountain-like cascade full.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost when nights turn mild.

directions: Surface-sow indoors on damp mix and keep the seed uncovered, warm, and moist through germination. This trailing Fountain selection carries deep crimson-red flowers with pale eyes for rich basket color. Water freely so stems never dry and clip midseason to draw out fresh trailing growth.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into baskets or bed edges.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto moist starting mix indoors and leave uncovered under light to germinate. This trailing Fountain color blooms clean white, spilling gracefully from baskets and boxes. Keep it well watered in part sun and shear back in midsummer to keep the white cascade coming.

planting.note: Plant out around the last frost date.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of a damp medium indoors and hold light and warmth until seedlings develop. This compact Palace selection forms tight cushions covered in deep blue flowers for crisp edging. Keep evenly moist in full sun and shear after the first flush to keep the mounds neat.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost with mild nights ahead.

directions: Surface-sow indoors and keep the seed uncovered, moist, and lit until it sprouts over a couple of weeks. This compact Palace color blooms white on low, even mounds for a bright, tidy border ribbon. Provide steady water and full sun, then trim midseason to renew the flowering cushions.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost since this heat-tolerant type is grown vegetatively.

directions: This vegetatively propagated selection is grown from cuttings and sold as plants, so set transplants at their original depth in rich, moist soil or basket mix. Bred for stronger heat tolerance, it keeps producing sky-blue flowers through summer when older seed types would stall. Water generously and trim lightly if it thins out to bring the trailing mounds back into full bloom.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out after frost; this vegetative type is not grown from seed.

directions: Grown from cuttings rather than seed, this heat-tolerant white form is planted from nursery transplants set at the same depth as their pots. Give it fertile, moisture-retentive mix and steady feeding to keep the pure white flowers coming through the warm months. Water often and shear if it stretches to restore a dense, blooming mound.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost, as this type is vegetatively raised.

directions: This vegetative selection comes as rooted plants, so set transplants at their container depth in rich, well-drained but moisture-holding mix. Its blue flowers each carry a bright white eye, and the improved heat tolerance keeps them opening deep into summer. Keep the roots damp, feed regularly, and clip lightly if needed to refresh the trailing habit.

planting.note: Set vegetative transplants out after the last frost.

directions: Sold as rooted plants from cuttings, this heat-tolerant form is set out at its pot depth in fertile, moisture-retentive soil or basket mix. It stands out with intense violet-purple flowers that keep coming through warm weather that stalls seed strains. Water freely and feed steadily, trimming lightly if the trailing stems begin to thin.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost; this series is grown vegetatively.

directions: This vegetative Techno Heat selection is raised from cuttings and sold as plants, so set transplants at their nursery depth in rich, evenly moist soil. Selected for heat endurance, it holds its saturated dark-blue flowers through summer conditions that fade many seed lobelias. Keep it watered and fed, and shear back midseason if it opens up to bring back full bloom.

planting.note: Set rooted plants out after frost; this heat series is vegetatively propagated.

directions: Grown from cuttings for its heat stamina, this trailing form is planted from transplants set at their original soil line in rich, moisture-holding mix. It pours out mid-blue flowers and keeps flowering through summer heat that shuts down seed-grown types. Water often, feed regularly, and trim lightly if the runners thin to restore the cascade.

planting.note: Plant vegetative transplants out after the last frost.

directions: This vegetative selection is sold as rooted plants, so set transplants at their pot depth in fertile, consistently moist soil or basket mix. Its large deep-blue flowers keep opening through summer thanks to good heat tolerance, making it a strong basket and container plant. Water generously and feed through the season, shearing lightly if it stretches to renew the mound.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost; this type is not seed-grown.

directions: Propagated from cuttings, this trailing selection is planted from transplants set at their container depth in rich, moisture-retentive mix. It carries deep dark-blue flowers and keeps a vigorous, well-branched habit through warm weather. Water often, feed steadily, and pinch back lightly to keep the cascading stems dense and blooming.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after frost, as this trailing type is grown vegetatively.

directions: This vegetative selection comes as rooted plants, so set transplants at their nursery depth in fertile, moisture-holding basket mix. Its long, cascading stems spill blue flowers in a waterfall effect ideal for tall baskets and window boxes. Water frequently so the trailing growth never dries, and shear midseason to keep the curtain of bloom full.

planting.note: Sow where its low sprawling stems can spill over edges and walls.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and firm it into contact with damp soil, leaving it uncovered so light can trigger germination. Let the loose, spreading habit cascade from raised beds or containers and trim lightly when flowering slows. White bloom clusters carry a strong sweet fragrance through the cool months.

planting.note: A classic edging strain that hugs the ground in a low white sheet.

directions: Sprinkle seed thinly on prepared soil and leave it exposed to light, misting to keep the surface damp. Grow it as a tight front-of-border ribbon and clip spent flowers to keep the carpet fresh. Snowy blooms open in roughly six weeks and often persist until hard frost.

planting.note: An improved low white type with tidy, uniform mounds.

directions: Broadcast seed over firmed soil and press gently without burying, since exposure to light aids sprouting. Water from below or mist to avoid washing the fine seed away, and shear after peak bloom for a second show. The compact plants blanket edges with white by early summer.

planting.note: Prized for unusually large, pure white florets on vigorous mounds.

directions: Surface-sow and leave uncovered, keeping the seedbed consistently moist during the germination week. Space slightly wider than dwarf strains to let the fuller plants breathe, and trim after flushes to maintain vigor. The oversized white clusters make a bold, fragrant edging.

planting.note: An extra-dwarf white selection for the tightest edgings and cracks.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp soil and leave it in the light to germinate quickly. Tuck it between pavers or along path edges where its low cushion stays neat. Sheets of tiny white blooms appear within about six weeks and rebloom after a light shearing.

planting.note: A low-spreading strain in rich violet-purple, a longtime favorite.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and firm it in without covering, providing steady moisture until sprouts show. Grow in full sun for the deepest color and shear lightly to renew the display. Fragrant purple carpets form in about seven weeks and draw pollinators all season.

planting.note: An award-winning dwarf strain in bright rose-pink.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist soil and leave uncovered so light can prompt germination. Keep it in a sunny, well-drained spot for the truest rose tone and trim after each flush. The low mats bloom heavily in cool weather and often self-sow lightly.

planting.note: A deep violet strain that reads dark against white companions.

directions: Sow the seed on the surface and press lightly, misting to hold moisture through germination. Site in full sun and shear back midseason to keep plants dense and flowering. Clouds of dark violet blooms carry a sweet honey scent from spring into fall.

planting.note: One of the darkest strains, in near-black purple.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto damp soil without burying and keep evenly moist until it sprouts. Grow in bright sun for the richest dark color and clip spent heads to prolong bloom. The low mounds of deep purple flowers pair strikingly with silver foliage.

planting.note: A compact strain in deep violet-blue tones.

directions: Surface-sow and leave the seed uncovered, watering gently to avoid displacing it. Keep plants in full sun and lightly shear after heavy flushes for repeat bloom. Dense mounds of dark violet-blue flowers make a fragrant low edging.

planting.note: A blended strain mixing white, rose, lavender, and violet.

directions: Broadcast the mixed seed over firmed soil and leave it exposed to light for even germination. Grow as a soft multicolor edging and shear midseason to freshen the planting. The low mounds open in shades from cream to deep purple with a honey scent.

planting.note: A taller, upright white selection valued for strong fragrance.

directions: Sow seed thinly on the surface, leaving it uncovered and keeping the bed moist while it germinates. Its slightly upright form suits mixed borders and cutting for small posies. Fragrant white sprays continue for weeks and rebound after a light trim.

planting.note: An early, compact series member in clean white.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp soil without covering and mist to keep it moist through sprouting. The quick, uniform habit fills containers and edges fast in cool weather. Sheets of white bloom arrive early and repeat after shearing.

planting.note: A compact early series member in vivid rose-pink.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist soil and leave uncovered so light can trigger germination. Grow in full sun for the boldest rose color and clip after flushes to renew bloom. The tidy mounds flower early and hold color best in cool conditions.

planting.note: A compact early series member in saturated purple.

directions: Press seed onto the soil surface and keep evenly moist, leaving it in the light to sprout. The fast, even habit suits massed edgings and mixed pots. Deep purple flower clusters open early and rebloom after a light shearing.

planting.note: An early, well-branched series in soft lavender.

directions: Sow on the surface and firm gently without burying, misting to keep moisture steady until germination. The dense, mounded plants fill baskets and edges quickly. Fragrant lavender flowers appear early and continue with occasional trimming.

planting.note: A compact, early series member in strong deep pink.

directions: Press the seed onto damp soil and leave it uncovered for light-driven germination. Grow in full sun and shear after the first heavy flush to keep it blooming. Rounded plants carry deep pink, honey-scented clusters through cool weather.

planting.note: A large-flowered series member with bright white blooms.

directions: Surface-sow and leave the seed exposed to light, keeping the bed moist until seedlings emerge. The vigorous, larger plants suit borders and mixed containers alike. Big white clusters bloom heavily and rebound quickly after a trim.

planting.note: A large-flowered series member in blended purple tones.

directions: Press seed onto firmed soil without covering and mist to maintain surface moisture. Grow in full sun for rich color on the fuller, more upright plants. Large purple-toned clusters bloom long and reflush after shearing.

planting.note: A large-flowered series member in soft lavender shades.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface, leaving it uncovered so light can prompt sprouting. The robust plants make a soft-toned mass for beds and baskets. Fragrant lavender clusters open freely and repeat after a light shearing.

planting.note: A compact, well-branched series offered in a color blend.

directions: Broadcast the mixed seed over moist soil and leave it exposed to light for even germination. The tidy, uniform plants suit packs, pots, and edgings. Expect a quick, multicolor haze of scented flowers that rebounds after trimming.

planting.note: A very compact, uniform series member in white for packs and edging.

directions: Press the seed onto damp soil and keep it uncovered and moist while it germinates. The dwarf, even habit is ideal for tight edgings and container fillers. White flower clusters cover the mounds early and rebloom after clipping.

planting.note: A dependable low white strain long used for edging and rock gardens.

directions: Surface-sow onto firmed soil and leave uncovered, misting to hold moisture through germination. Grow along paths and among stones where its low mounds knit together. Fragrant white sheets bloom in about six weeks and refresh after shearing.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost; this sterile hybrid keeps blooming through summer heat.

directions: Plant nursery-grown transplants at the same depth they grew in the pot, since this seedless hybrid is propagated from cuttings rather than seed. Its vigorous, heat-tolerant habit spreads wide and needs no deadheading to keep flowering. Expect a continuous cascade of fragrant white blooms from spring until frost.

planting.note: A vigorous sterile hybrid whose white flowers age to a soft pink blush.

directions: Transplant rooted starts after the last frost, planting level with the surrounding soil. This cutting-grown hybrid sets no seed, so it flowers nonstop without deadheading through heat. Blooms open white and take on a delicate pink flush as they mature.

planting.note: A variegated sterile hybrid with cream-edged foliage and white flowers.

directions: Set out rooted transplants after frost at their original planting depth, as this hybrid is grown from cuttings. The cream-margined leaves add interest even between flushes, and no deadheading is needed. White flower clusters appear over the variegated mounds from spring through fall.

planting.note: A trailing hybrid in lavender-purple, well suited to baskets.

directions: Plant rooted transplants after the last frost at the same depth they grew in their cells, since this hybrid is propagated by cuttings. Let its trailing stems spill from hanging baskets and window boxes with no deadheading required. Fragrant lavender-purple flowers keep coming from spring into autumn.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and set out young plants while nights are still cool.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a fine, moist mix and barely cover, since light aids germination. Grow the seedlings cool and set them out into rich, well-drained soil once the weather settles. Dense, sweetly clove-scented spikes open about ten weeks after sowing, so keep plants deadheaded to extend the show.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for compact plants ready to plant after the hardest frosts pass.

directions: Surface-sow into cell trays and keep the mix lightly moist and bright until sprouts appear. This dwarf, well-branched strain carries a high share of fully double flowers and stays tidy in pots or the front of a bed. Expect fragrant, densely packed spikes in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Raise indoors and transplant while cool spring weather still holds.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of moist starter mix and do not bury it, as it needs light to sprout. This compact, freely branching series holds up well in containers and produces a strong proportion of double blooms in soft antique shades. Cut spikes for the vase once the lower florets open.

planting.note: Start early indoors so tall single-stem plants can finish before summer heat.

directions: Surface-sow and grow the seedlings cool and evenly bright for straight, sturdy stems. This columnar cut-flower type makes a single long flowering spike, so space plants close and avoid pinching. Harvest when about a third of the florets on the stem have opened.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; this series tolerates a slightly wider temperature range for cutting.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and hold the mix moist and bright until germination. Grow as a single-stem cut flower without pinching, spacing plants tightly for long, straight spikes. Cut the stem when the lowest flowers open for the longest vase life.

planting.note: Sow indoors early; this F1 series sets buds without needing a long cold spell.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and keep bright, as covering the seed slows sprouting. This improved hybrid flowers reliably in a shorter, warmer window than old strains, making it forgiving for spring plantings. Enjoy the fat, fragrant double spikes and shear lightly after the first flush.

planting.note: Start indoors well ahead of planting so the tall spikes mature in cool weather.

directions: Press the seed into the surface of moist mix and leave it exposed to light. This non-branching column type throws one tall, densely flowered spike ideal for cutting, so do not pinch the growing tip. Harvest the whole stem when the base florets first open.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and set out compact plants once frosts ease.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it moist and lit until it germinates. This short, bushy strain suits edging and pots and carries clove-scented double flowers over a low mound. Deadhead spent spikes to keep the plants neat and blooming.

planting.note: Raise indoors for early, dwarf plants to line beds and borders.

directions: Sow on the surface of a light mix and press gently without covering. This very compact strain flowers quickly and fills small containers and edgings with fragrance. Trim off finished spikes to tidy the plant and encourage side shoots.

planting.note: Sow in mid to late summer, overwinter young plants, and bloom the following spring.

directions: Sow in summer and grow the plants on so they enter winter as sturdy leafy rosettes. Where winters are mild, overwinter them outdoors with light protection; elsewhere shelter them in a cold frame. After cold weather passes they send up tall, richly scented spring spikes.

planting.note: Start indoors early; this strain lets growers rogue out singles at the seedling stage for all-double crops.

directions: Surface-sow and germinate cool, then chill seedlings briefly so the double-flowered plants show pale, notched leaves. Remove the darker-leaved singles and grow on only the light-leaved doubles for a uniform cut crop. Fast to finish, it opens fragrant spikes roughly seven weeks after transplanting.

planting.note: Start indoors; this heirloom branching strain thrives with a long, cool growing spell.

directions: Press seed onto moist mix and leave uncovered so light can reach it. This old branching strain builds a bushy plant with many fragrant side spikes rather than one column. Give it cool weather and steady moisture, then cut stems as the lower flowers open.

planting.note: Sow indoors early so the large branching plants finish before hot weather.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and keep the tray moist and bright until sprouting. This vigorous branching strain grows tall with several long, scented spikes good for cutting. Feed lightly, keep cool, and harvest when the bottom florets first open.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant into cool spring soil.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of moist mix and press without burying. This branching bedding strain offers a soft mix of colors on fragrant double and semi-double spikes. Deadhead regularly and the plants keep flowering through the cool part of the season.

planting.note: Start indoors early so these tall cutting spikes mature during cool weather.

directions: Surface-sow and grow seedlings cool and evenly bright for long, straight stems. This series is bred for greenhouse and field cut-flower production, giving tall single spikes in a wide color range. Cut when a few of the lowest flowers open for the best vase performance.

planting.note: Sow directly where it is to grow around the last frost and make repeat sowings for continuous scent.

directions: Sow seed thinly about a quarter inch deep directly in the garden and thin the seedlings once they are established. The small, unassuming lilac flowers stay closed by day and release a powerful sweet fragrance at dusk. Plant it near paths, doors, and seating so the evening perfume drifts where you sit.

planting.note: Direct-sow around the last frost and sow again every few weeks for lasting evening fragrance.

directions: Sow shallowly straight into the ground and keep the soil lightly moist until seedlings appear. This mixed strain of night-scented stock carries small flowers in a range of soft pastel tones that perfume the air after dark. Grow it beside patios and windows to catch the heady evening scent.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out once the danger of hard frost passes.

directions: Press the dust-fine seed onto the surface of moist mix without covering it, since light aids sprouting, and keep humidity high until seedlings appear. Grow in consistently damp, rich soil and shelter plants from hot afternoon sun so they do not stall. Expect the first trumpet-shaped, often speckled blooms roughly ten weeks from sowing, with flowering strongest during cool, moist spells.

planting.note: Sow indoors in flats and transplant compact seedlings after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and leave it uncovered under bright light, misting to keep the top layer damp. This early, uniform strain forms tidy mounds that bloom quickly, so pinch spent flowers to prolong the show. Keep the roots cool and evenly moist, and watch for a dense flush of two-toned blooms in mid to late spring.

planting.note: Raise transplants indoors and plant out when frost risk has passed.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface of a peaty mix and firm it gently without burying it. This compact, well-branched series thrives in damp shade and containers where the soil never dries out. Deadhead lightly and feed every couple of weeks to keep the bright, blotched flowers coming through the cooler months.

planting.note: Start under cover late winter and harden off before planting out.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist seed compost and keep in bright, warm conditions without covering the seed. This dwarf selection carries solid, deep-red trumpets and stays neat at the front of a bed or in a damp trough. Keep the soil reliably moist and cool, and pinch off faded blooms to encourage a long succession of flowers.

planting.note: Grow transplants indoors and set out after frost has passed.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix and leave it exposed to light to trigger germination. This compact strain produces clear yellow flowers, often lightly speckled in the throat, on tidy plants that suit edging and pots. Provide steady moisture and light shade in warm regions so the display holds up through late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter for planting out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist compost and keep bright and humid until seedlings emerge. This dwarf form offers soft pink trumpets on compact plants that shine in shaded containers and border fronts. Keep the root zone cool and damp, deadhead regularly, and enjoy waves of bloom while conditions stay mild.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Leave the tiny seed uncovered on damp mix and hold at warm room temperature under good light. This mix blends yellows, reds, oranges, and heavily spotted faces on quick, bushy plants. Grow in moist, fertile soil with midday shade in hot climates, and shear lightly if plants tire to spark a fresh round of bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface and do not cover, keeping the tray moist and brightly lit. This award-winning selection bears large golden-yellow flowers boldly blotched with maroon-red in the throat. Grow in rich, damp soil that never dries out and remove old blooms to keep the vivid display going through the cool season.

planting.note: Sow indoors in early spring for compact transplants after frost.

directions: Surface-sow and press in lightly without burying, keeping the seed exposed to light. This very dwarf, fast series flowers early on mounded plants ideal for small pots and edging. Water freely to keep soil moist and cool, and expect a dense carpet of speckled blooms in a wide color range within about eight weeks of sowing.

planting.note: Start under cover and transplant after the last frost date.

directions: Leave the fine seed on the surface of moist compost and keep humid and bright until it germinates. This vigorous strain is grown for its unusually large, richly patterned flowers on well-branched plants. Keep the soil consistently damp and provide afternoon shade in warm gardens, deadheading to sustain the big, showy blooms.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors or direct-sow at the edge of ponds and wet ground in spring.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto wet soil without covering, as it needs light and constant moisture to sprout. This adaptable native thrives along stream margins, seeps, and boggy ground where its roots stay saturated. Bright yellow, red-flecked flowers appear from late spring onward, and plants often self-sow freely in damp sites.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out into moist ground after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on damp mix under light, keeping it moist through germination. This western native forms leafy clumps in wet soil and bears tubular scarlet flowers that draw hummingbirds. Grow it beside water or in a bog bed, cut back tired stems in summer, and expect bloom from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Cold-moist stratify seed, then start indoors before planting out.

directions: Chill the seed moist for a few weeks, then surface-sow under light on a damp, gritty mix. This mountain native enjoys cool, wet meadows and streamsides and dislikes prolonged heat and drought. Rose-pink flowers with yellow-lined throats open through summer; keep soil moist and give it a cool root run.

planting.note: Start seed or set out nursery plants in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed under light, then grow the seedlings on in gritty, sharply drained mix. Unlike the moisture-loving hybrids, this evergreen California native tolerates drought and resents wet winter soil. It carries sticky leaves and orange-to-apricot flowers from spring into summer; shear lightly after the main flush to keep it compact.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, and plant into wet ground after frost.

directions: Give the seed a cold-moist chill, then surface-sow on wet mix and keep in light. This hardy eastern native grows in marshes, wet meadows, and pond edges with its feet in damp soil. Slender stems carry lavender-blue, snapdragon-like flowers through summer that attract bumblebees; it self-sows where the ground stays moist.

planting.note: Surface-sow indoors or direct-sow onto moist soil in spring.

directions: Press the dust-like seed onto wet soil and leave uncovered, keeping constantly moist. This low, spreading species has soft, sticky foliage and pale yellow flowers over a long season. Grow it in a damp, shady spot or bog garden where it can creep and trail; keep the soil from ever drying out.

planting.note: Start indoors or direct-sow onto wet ground once frost danger eases.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on the surface of damp soil and do not cover it. This Chilean species and its garden forms carry bright yellow flowers often marked with bold red blotches. Grow at pond margins and in wet, rich soil where it can spread; deadhead or let it self-seed for a continuing display.

planting.note: Chill seed briefly, then surface-sow indoors for a spring planting.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed on damp, gritty mix and keep cool and moist under light. This diminutive alpine forms low mats of small rosettes rooting as they creep across wet ground. Bright yellow flowers rise on short stalks in summer; grow it in a boggy trough or streamside where it never dries.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out after frost; often grown as an annual.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix under light and keep humid until it germinates. This tender Chilean species bears pale rose-pink flowers with a creamy throat dusted in fine spots. Grow in cool, damp, rich soil and shelter from harsh sun; treat it as a half-hardy annual in colder gardens and enjoy bloom from early summer.

planting.note: Set out cutting-grown plants in spring into fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this cutting-propagated selection in gritty, sharply drained soil and water sparingly once established. It forms a bushy evergreen mound covered in golden-yellow flowers over a long season. Trim back after the main spring flush to keep it dense and coax repeat bloom; avoid soggy winter soil, which quickly rots the roots.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown stock in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Grow this compact, cutting-raised selection in lean, fast-draining soil with full sun. It produces a heavy show of rich red to orange-red flowers on a tidy, low mound. Water moderately during establishment then keep it on the dry side, and shear lightly after flowering to maintain shape and prompt another flush.

planting.note: Set cutting-grown plants out in spring into sharply drained ground.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and let it dry between waterings once rooted in. This shrubby monkeyflower bears masses of warm pumpkin-orange flowers over a long spring-to-summer season. Cut it back by about a third after the main bloom to keep it bushy, and protect from hard frost in colder gardens.

planting.note: Direct sow when frost danger has passed and soil is warm.

directions: Sow the pea-sized seeds half an inch deep after soaking them overnight to speed sprouting. This strain produces flowers streaked and splashed with contrasting colors, and a single plant may show several patterns at once. Blooms open in the afternoon and release a sweet scent that draws evening moths.

planting.note: Sow directly outdoors once nights stay reliably warm.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep in a sunny bed with average, well-drained soil. This compact selection carries large marbled blooms swirled in two tones, often red and white or yellow and rose. Deadheading is optional since the bushy plants rebloom freely until the first hard frost.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in full sun to develop the brightest foliage color.

directions: Sow half an inch deep once the soil is warm and give the plant full sun to keep the foliage vivid. This form is prized for its chartreuse to lime-yellow leaves that contrast against magenta trumpet flowers. The leaf color is strongest in bright light, so avoid heavy shade.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors a month before planting out.

directions: Sow the seeds about half an inch deep and keep the bed evenly moist until sprouts appear. This mixture blends solid shades of red, rose, yellow, and white on vigorous, rounded plants. Flowers unfurl in mid-afternoon and perfume the garden through the evening hours.

planting.note: Sow outdoors once the ground has warmed after the last spring frost.

directions: Nick or soak the tough seed coat and sow half an inch deep in a warm, sunny spot. This color selection bears uniform deep crimson-magenta trumpets on a bushy plant. Blooms open each afternoon and close by the following morning, repeating from summer into fall.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start early indoors in cool climates.

directions: Sow the round black seeds half an inch deep in average garden soil after soaking overnight. This form produces clear lemon-yellow flowers, sometimes lightly flecked with rose. Cut the plant back if it sprawls, and enjoy fresh blooms opening every afternoon until frost.

planting.note: Sow when soil is warm and settled after the final frost.

directions: Plant seeds half an inch deep in full sun and water moderately through the growing season. The pure white flowers glow at dusk and are especially fragrant, making this a favorite for evening and moon gardens. Blooms open in late afternoon and are pollinated by night-flying moths.

planting.note: Direct sow into warm soil after all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow half an inch deep in a sunny bed and thin seedlings to stand about a foot apart. This selection carries soft rose-pink trumpets on a rounded, shrubby plant. Flowers open in the afternoon heat and continue reliably until cold weather ends the season.

planting.note: Sow after frost, or start indoors six weeks early for a longer bloom season.

directions: Sow the seeds half an inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and be patient, as germination can be uneven. Each bloom has an unusually long slender tube, up to six inches, opening white with a pink or violet throat and long protruding stamens. The intensely fragrant flowers open in the evening and are pollinated by hawk moths.

planting.note: Sow scarified seed in spring; plants form a deep taproot and dislike transplanting once established.

directions: Scarify the hard seed and sow half an inch deep in lean, fast-draining soil where the plant can stay put. This drought-tough Southwestern native forms a wide sprawling mound from a large woody root and needs little supplemental water once rooted. Masses of magenta trumpet flowers open in late afternoon and close by mid-morning through summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring into warm, gritty soil in full sun.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep in sharply drained soil and site it in full sun with minimal irrigation. This slender Southwestern native produces narrow tubular scarlet flowers that attract hummingbirds. Blooms appear through the warm months and open in the cooler part of the day.

planting.note: Sow in spring; cold stratification of fall-sown seed improves germination.

directions: Sow seed a quarter to half an inch deep in ordinary well-drained soil; fall sowing lets winter cold break dormancy. This tough prairie native has heart-shaped leaves and clusters of small pink to magenta flowers held in papery cups. Flowers open in the afternoon, and the deep taproot makes established plants very drought tolerant.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out around the last spring frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and barely cover, keeping it cool and lightly moist until sprouts appear in one to three weeks. Pinch young stems once to build bushy plants and grow on in bright, cool conditions. Expect a rush of pouched, jewel-toned blooms in late spring; shear spent flowers to prompt a second flush before summer heat.

planting.note: Raise seedlings under cover and transplant once frost danger passes.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface of a moist seed tray and leave it uncovered in a cool, bright spot. Move seedlings to gritty, free-draining soil in full sun and water steadily without waterlogging. Small two-lipped flowers in mixed pastel and bright shades open through spring; trim after the first flush to extend the show.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out after the last frost for spring bloom.

directions: Sow the fine seed thinly on moist mix, leaving it uncovered and cool until germination. Grow seedlings on in bright light and pinch once to encourage side shoots. Dainty white to pale blue flowers carry a soft honey scent in spring; deadhead regularly to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant into moist, well-drained soil and pinch the tips early to build a low, spreading mound. Water in dry spells and shear lightly after the main flush to keep it tidy and blooming. Clouds of small lilac-pink flowers appear from late spring into summer; take stem cuttings in late summer to overwinter frost-free.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter or plant rooted cuttings after frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed on cool, moist mix or set out rooted cuttings once frost has passed. Grow in full sun with steady moisture and pinch to keep the airy stems bushy. Slender spikes of soft blue to lavender flowers bloom over a long season; cut back by a third midsummer to renew growth.

planting.note: Raise from seed under cover or plant rooted cuttings after the last frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of a cool, damp tray, or plant young rooted stems into free-draining soil. Keep evenly moist through the growing season and trim spent flowers to prolong bloom. Delicate mauve, white, or pink flowers cover the plant from late spring; take cuttings to carry favorites through winter.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and plant out around the last frost.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist mix and keep cool and lightly shaded until seedlings emerge. Pinch once and grow on in bright, airy conditions before setting out in fertile soil. This compact mix opens in a jumble of red, yellow, orange, pink, and blue through spring; shear after flowering for a repeat display.

planting.note: Raise indoors from a late-winter sowing and transplant after frost.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix and leave uncovered in a cool, bright place until it sprouts. Grow seedlings on steadily and pinch to promote branching before planting out. The crisp blue-and-white bicolor blooms, named for the Dutch airline's colors, cover the plant in spring; deadhead to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: Start seed under cover and set out young plants after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist seed compost and keep cool and evenly damp until germination. Pinch seedlings once and grow on in good light before planting into fertile soil. Bold red-and-white flowers, sometimes sold as Danish Flag, make a striking spring show; shear spent blooms to encourage more.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and plant out around the last frost date.

directions: Sprinkle the fine seed on moist mix and leave uncovered in a cool, bright spot to germinate. Pinch to shape and harden off before setting into free-draining soil in full sun. A cheerful blend of warm sunset shades opens in spring; trim the plants back after the first flush for fresh growth.

planting.note: Raise indoors and transplant out once frost has passed.

directions: Press seed lightly onto cool, damp compost and keep bright but not hot until sprouts show. Grow the compact seedlings on and pinch once for density before planting out. Masses of clear sky-blue flowers smother this dwarf form in spring, making it ideal for edging and containers; deadhead to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on moist mix and keep cool and lightly moist during germination. Pinch seedlings and grow on in bright light before setting into rich, well-drained soil. Vivid scarlet-red flowers make a hot-toned spring display; shear back after the first burst to renew flowering.

planting.note: Sow under cover and transplant seedlings after frost danger passes.

directions: Scatter seed on damp compost and leave uncovered in a cool, well-lit spot until it germinates. Pinch once and harden off before planting into fertile, moisture-retentive soil. Bright crimson-and-white bicolor flowers open freely in spring; remove faded blooms to keep the plant productive.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after the last frost once the soil has warmed.

directions: Set young plants into moist, well-drained soil and pinch early to form a spreading cushion. Keep watered in dry weather and trim after the first flush to maintain shape. Airy sprays of soft pink flowers appear from late spring well into summer; take cuttings in late summer to overwinter under cover.

planting.note: Plant out rooted cuttings after the last frost has passed.

directions: Plant into fertile, free-draining soil or containers and pinch the tips to encourage branching. Water regularly and shear back lightly after heavy flushes to keep it blooming. Creamy white flowers pour out a rich vanilla fragrance from late spring; propagate from cuttings in late summer to keep this sterile hybrid going.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost, once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant into moisture-retentive but well-drained soil and pinch early for a bushy habit. Keep evenly watered and cut back by a third after the first flush to renew growth. Pure white flowers with a soft scent bloom over a long season; take late-summer cuttings to carry the plant through winter frost-free.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after the last frost.

directions: Set young plants into free-draining soil in full sun and pinch to build a full, mounded shape. Water steadily and shear midseason to prevent legginess and refresh bloom. Soft lavender-blue flowers with pale eyes appear over many weeks from late spring; propagate by cuttings to keep the color true.

planting.note: Transplant rooted stock after the last frost.

directions: Plant into rich, well-drained soil or pots and pinch the growing tips to encourage bushiness. Water regularly and trim after peak bloom to keep the plant compact and flowering. Two-tone flowers in raspberry-pink and cream carry a light scent through the season; take cuttings in late summer for the following year.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants once frost has passed.

directions: Plant into moist, free-draining soil and pinch early to build a dense, rounded mound. Water consistently and shear lightly after the main flush to prompt repeat bloom. Soft mauve-pink flowers with a gentle fragrance appear over a long spring-to-summer season; propagate by cuttings to overwinter frost-free.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after the last frost.

directions: Set plants into fertile, well-drained soil and pinch the tips for a fuller habit. Keep watered in dry spells and cut back after flushes to maintain a neat shape. Deep raspberry-pink flowers with a sweet scent bloom freely from late spring; take late-summer cuttings to keep this hybrid year to year.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock outdoors after the last frost.

directions: Plant into rich, well-drained soil or containers and pinch once for a mounded, spreading habit. Water regularly and give a light trim after heavy bloom to keep it flowering. Rich cranberry-red flowers cover the plant from spring through summer where nights stay cool; propagate by cuttings since seed is not offered.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant into free-draining soil or pots and pinch the tips early for a full, rounded shape. Keep evenly moist and shear lightly after the main flush to renew flowering. Cheerful pale yellow flowers pour out over a long, cool season; increase your stock from cuttings rather than seed.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out once frost danger passes.

directions: Set young plants into fertile, well-drained soil and pinch to build a mounded, spreading form. Water steadily and trim after flushes to keep bloom coming through the season. Warm orange flowers brushed with deeper tones cover the plant in cool weather; propagate by cuttings to keep the color true.

planting.note: Transplant rooted plants after the last frost.

directions: Plant into well-drained soil or containers and pinch once for density and spread. Keep watered in dry spells and shear back lightly after peak bloom to refresh the plant. Soft pink flowers open in abundance through the cool months of the season; raise new plants from cuttings rather than seed.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and plant out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed onto warm, moist mix and keep bright until seedlings appear. Grow on in cooler conditions and pinch once before planting into well-drained soil. Fragrant pure-white flowers cover the compact plants from spring into early summer; deadhead or shear to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Raise indoors from a late-winter sowing and transplant after frost.

directions: Press seed lightly onto warm, damp compost and keep bright and evenly moist until germination. Move seedlings to cooler, airy conditions and pinch once before planting out. Fragrant rose-pink flowers bloom on tidy mounds from spring into summer; trim spent flowers to encourage repeat bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring, or in autumn in frost-free winter areas for earlier bloom.

directions: Press seed lightly into cool, moist soil and barely cover it, then keep the bed from drying out until sprouts appear. Space the low mounding plants a few inches apart and shelter them from hot afternoon sun in warm regions. Each white cup is marked with a violet blotch at the tip of every petal, giving the five-spotted bloom its name from late spring onward.

planting.note: Sow in place in early spring while the soil is still cool and moist.

directions: Sow the fine seed directly into loose soil and cover with a thin blanket of earth to block light. Water gently and regularly, thinning young plants so air moves freely between them. This white-flowered form is dusted with tiny dark speckles toward the center and blooms within a couple of months of sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring, or fall sow in mild-winter gardens for a spring display.

directions: Sprinkle seed over prepared soil, cover lightly, and firm gently to ensure good contact. Keep evenly moist during germination and thin the low, spreading plants to a few inches apart. The near-black petals are rimmed with a crisp white edge, making a striking low carpet from late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Sow directly where plants will flower in early spring as soil warms slightly.

directions: Cover the seed with a shallow layer of soil since it germinates best in the dark, and keep the surface damp. Thin seedlings once established and give them cool, bright conditions rather than baking heat. The white flowers are finely peppered with black or deep purple dots and appear within about two months of sowing.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or very early spring; this native reseeds readily in woodland-edge gardens.

directions: Sow seed on moist, humus-rich soil and cover lightly, favoring a spot with morning sun and afternoon shade. Keep the bed watered until plants are up, then let them fill in and lean on nearby stems for support. This south-central US native bears larger lavender-blue flowers with pale centers in spring and self-sows for the following year.

planting.note: Scatter seed in early spring in cool, moist ground; good in wildflower mixes and rock gardens.

directions: Broadcast the small seed over damp soil and rake in lightly, keeping the surface moist until sprouts show. This ground-hugging mat spreader wants cool weather and steady moisture rather than heat or drought. Tiny white to pale-blue flowers, often veined and tipped with a dark spot, dot the low foliage in spring.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter and set plants out after frost danger passes.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto the surface of moist mix without covering it, since light aids sprouting. Move seedlings out after the soil warms and keep them evenly watered through summer. The tubular white flowers open toward evening and release a strong sweet scent that peaks around dusk.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for transplants ready once frost has passed.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of a moist tray and leave it uncovered for light. Harden off and plant out after the last frost, spacing to allow air movement. The unusual chartreuse-green blooms carry well in borders and cutting arrangements from midsummer onward.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors and transplant when nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Surface-sow indoors under bright light and mist to keep the medium damp. Set plants in fertile soil after frost, then deadhead spent spikes to prolong flowering. Large fragrant white trumpets scent the garden most strongly in the evening.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors early to give this tall species a full season.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist seed mix and keep warm and bright until sprouts appear. Give each plant room, rich soil, and steady moisture to support the dramatic height. Tall stems carry drooping clusters of long white tubular flowers that perfume the air after dark.

planting.note: Start indoors well before the season since this selection matures large.

directions: Press seed onto damp mix and leave it exposed to light to germinate. Transplant into deep fertile ground after frost and water generously in dry spells. A single specimen makes a bold statement, topped with fragrant white pendant trumpets from midsummer to fall.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and plant out once frost risk has ended.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep it lit and moist until germination. Move plants to a sunny spot with good soil after the last frost date. Airy stems bear nodding apple-green bells with blue pollen, prized by hummingbirds through the warm months.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early; variegated seedlings benefit from a long season.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist mix and provide bright light for germination. Set plants out after frost in fertile, well-drained soil and keep evenly watered. Cream-marbled foliage sets off the same nodding green bells as the species from summer into autumn.

planting.note: Give this tall Brazilian species an early indoor start for best bloom.

directions: Press the dust-fine seed onto damp mix and keep it warm and bright. Transplant into rich soil after frost and stake tall stems in exposed sites. Wiry branched panicles carry small flowers that open white and age through pink, giving a shifting cloud of color.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for bedding plants ready after frost.

directions: Scatter seed thinly on the surface and do not cover, as light triggers sprouting. Grow on in bright conditions and plant out into beds once nights warm. This garden hybrid produces upward-facing flowers in many shades that stay open through the day.

planting.note: Start indoors and transplant compact plants once frost has passed.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and leave uncovered under bright light. Move the tidy seedlings into beds or containers after frost, spacing closely for a full look. This dwarf series bears weather-tolerant upfacing blooms in a wide color range all summer.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and plant out after the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of damp mix and keep it lit until it germinates. Set plants in sun to light shade after frost and water during dry stretches. Bred for fragrance, this series carries scented upfacing flowers that stay open into the evening.

planting.note: Sow indoors a few weeks ahead for quick, uniform bedding plants.

directions: Leave the tiny seed on the surface of moist mix for light germination. Grow the very compact plants on and set them out closely after frost. This early dwarf series flowers fast and holds neat mounds of color in beds and pots.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and transplant after frost danger.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep bright and moist until seedlings emerge. Plant out into beds or containers once the weather settles and frost has ended. This mid-height series offers fragrant flowers in reds, pinks, and white over a long season.

planting.note: Begin seed indoors for compact container-ready plants.

directions: Surface-sow onto damp mix and leave uncovered for light. Harden off and plant the low mounds into pots and edging after frost. This very dwarf series flowers early and stays neat, making it well suited to small containers.

planting.note: Sow indoors and plant out compact seedlings after frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep it moist and bright until sprouting. Set the tidy plants closely into beds or containers once frost passes. This early dwarf series holds well-branched color low to the ground all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early since these plants grow taller than dwarf series.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface and leave it exposed to light. Transplant into fertile soil after frost and give room for the taller stems. Airy sprays of rose and mauve flowers wave above the foliage from midsummer into fall.

planting.note: Raise indoors from late winter and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and keep bright until seedlings appear. Move plants into sunny, fertile ground after the last frost and water steadily. Slender stems bear nodding rose-pink blooms with lime-green backs and shimmering blue pollen loved by pollinators.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and set plants out once frost risk ends.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of damp mix and keep it lit for germination. Transplant into rich soil after frost and stake taller stems if needed. Tall airy sprays carry unusual dusky brown and chocolate-toned flowers through summer and fall.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter and plant out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and leave it uncovered under bright light. Move seedlings into sunny, well-drained beds after the last frost. Branching stems carry masses of small deep rose-red bells that draw bees and butterflies all season.

planting.note: Give this tall species an early indoor start for a full season of bloom.

directions: Sow on the surface of moist mix and keep warm and lit until germination. Transplant into fertile soil after frost and allow space for the branching stems. Loose panicles of small yellow-green tubular flowers rise well above the foliage and attract pollinators.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter and plant out after frost passes.

directions: Press the fine seed onto damp mix and keep it exposed to light. Set plants into sunny, well-drained soil after the last frost. This Australian species opens white starry flowers that release a sweet fragrance in the evening.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant once nights stay warm.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and provide bright light for germination. Move plants into sunny, fertile ground after frost and keep them watered. Very long slender white tubes open in the evening and give off a strong sweet scent for night pollinators.

planting.note: Raise seedlings indoors and transplant well after all frost has passed.

directions: Sprinkle the dust-fine seed on the surface of warm moist mix and leave it uncovered. Set the frost-sensitive plants into rich soil after the weather has fully warmed. Large leaves rise on a tall stalk topped by clusters of pink tubular flowers by late summer.

planting.note: Start indoors early and plant out only once frost danger has ended.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed onto warm moist mix and keep it lit. Transplant into fertile ground after the last frost and water through dry weather. This sturdy species carries broad leaves and short clusters of greenish-yellow tubular flowers by late summer.

planting.note: In frost-prone areas grow as a fast annual from an early indoor sowing.

directions: Sow on the surface of warm moist mix and leave uncovered for light. Plant in a hot, well-drained spot after frost, where it grows quickly into a tall shrubby form. Blue-green leaves back long-lasting sprays of tubular yellow flowers that draw hummingbirds; it may be weedy in mild climates.

planting.note: Sow indoors in late winter for scented plants ready after frost.

directions: Leave the tiny seed on the surface of damp mix for light germination. Transplant into sun or light shade after frost and deadhead to extend bloom. Tall stems open pure white trumpets that release a powerful sweet fragrance in the evening.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant compact seedlings out after frost.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and keep bright until seedlings emerge. Set the low plants into beds after frost for a solid block of color. This old-fashioned bedding form holds upfacing rich crimson flowers through the summer.

planting.note: Start seed indoors about two months before the last spring frost and set plants out once the soil has warmed.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and press it into moist mix without covering, since light aids germination. Grow on in full sun with even moisture and pinch young plants once to encourage a bushy mound. Shear lightly after the first flush to keep the cup-shaped blooms coming from early summer to frost.

planting.note: Set out young plants after frost as a spreading mat along paths or between stepping stones.

directions: Plant this low creeper in gritty, well-drained soil where its stems can root as they run. Water steadily through the first season while the mat knits together, then let it settle in. Expect a long parade of upward-facing white cups from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Raise from seed indoors and transplant after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of a moist, well-drained mix and keep it warm and bright until sprouts appear. Grow in full sun and pinch the slender stems early for a fuller, well-branched plant. Lavender-blue cups open steadily through the warm months and shrug off summer heat.

planting.note: Start indoors early and move out after frost; often treated as an annual in cold regions.

directions: Press the seed onto damp mix and leave it uncovered under bright light to germinate. Give the plant full sun and light, free-draining soil, and trim leggy growth to keep the woody-based stems tidy. Pale bluish cups appear from summer into autumn on the branching upper stems.

planting.note: Sow indoors well before the last frost for early bloom, then transplant into warm soil.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface and mist to settle it, keeping the tray bright and evenly moist. This compact white-flowered selection needs no staking and mounds neatly in beds or containers. Snowy cups with yellow throats blanket the plant from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Begin indoors about ten weeks ahead of the last frost and set out when the soil warms.

directions: Press the fine seed into moist mix without burying it, as light speeds sprouting. This low, spreading selection carries deep violet-blue cups over a tidy green mound. Deadhead or shear lightly in midsummer to refresh the display, which runs until frost.

planting.note: Raise indoors and transplant after frost; this series is bred for uniform, early flowering.

directions: Surface-sow onto a warm, moist mix and keep it bright until the seedlings show. This compact series selection holds a neat habit ideal for edging and pots. Blue-violet cups cover the plant early and continue steadily through the season.

planting.note: Start under lights early and move to warm soil once frost has passed.

directions: Press seed lightly onto damp mix and leave uncovered beneath bright light. This tidy white form pairs well with its blue siblings for a mixed edging. Clean white cups open early and keep coming until autumn frost.

planting.note: Sow indoors for early color and transplant into warm garden soil.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on the surface and keep it warm and evenly moist to germinate. This selection carries soft sky-blue cups over a compact, self-branching mound. Expect continuous bloom from early summer through frost with occasional light shearing.

planting.note: Set out vegetative plants after frost; this hybrid is grown from cuttings rather than seed.

directions: Plant this vegetatively propagated hybrid in full sun and well-drained soil once the weather has settled. Its trailing, mounding habit spills nicely from baskets and container edges. Lavender-blue cups smother the plant all summer and need little more than the occasional trim.

planting.note: Sow indoors ahead of frost and transplant to warm soil for a low white carpet.

directions: Press the fine seed onto moist mix and leave it uncovered under good light. This is the white-flowered counterpart to Purple Robe, forming the same low, spreading mound. Shear lightly at midseason to keep the pale cups flowing until frost.

planting.note: Sow directly where plants are to flower, in early spring or autumn.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep into a sunny, well-drained bed and keep evenly moist until sprouts appear. Because it resents being moved, always sow it in its final position and thin rather than transplant. This heirloom bears semi-double cornflower-blue flowers on airy stems, excellent for cutting when buds are just opening.

planting.note: Direct sow into open ground in spring or fall; avoid starting indoors.

directions: Broadcast the fine seed over raked soil and press in lightly rather than burying deep. Thin the crowded seedlings so each has room to branch, and stake taller clumps in windy spots. Pure white flowers sit in a lacy green ruff, and leaving spent blooms yields ornamental striped pods for dried arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in place in early spring or in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Sow shallowly into loose, sunny soil and firm gently so seed stays in contact with the ground. Water during dry spells but let the soil surface dry between drinks, since Nigella tolerates lean conditions. Soft rose-pink flowers rise on wiry stems in early summer and make long-lasting cut flowers picked at first color.

planting.note: Direct sow into the flowering bed in spring, or in fall in warm zones.

directions: Sow the seed thinly and just cover it, keeping the bed moist until the ferny seedlings emerge. Thin to a few inches apart and avoid rich feeding, which encourages foliage over flowers. This popular blend produces blooms in shades of blue, pink, rose, and white, giving a jewel-toned drift and decorative seed pods.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or autumn; a fall sowing gives the largest plants.

directions: Direct sow into a sunny, free-draining spot and thin generously since this is a tall, branching selection. Provide light support such as twiggy stakes to keep the long stems upright. Deep navy-blue double flowers top statuesque plants and mature into large, richly veined pods that dry beautifully.

planting.note: Direct sow where plants will bloom, in early spring or fall.

directions: Scatter seed over prepared soil and cover only lightly, watering gently to settle it in. Thin seedlings and let plants self-support by growing in a close-knit drift. Flowers open soft pink and deepen to rich mulberry as they age, and the seed pods flush with the same dusky tone.

planting.note: Sow directly into containers or the front of a border in spring or fall.

directions: Sow seed shallowly and thin to a few inches apart, as this compact form stays low and tidy. It suits pots, edging, and rockeries where taller Nigella would flop. Small sky-blue flowers appear early above fine foliage, followed by proportionally small papery pods.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; grown as much for pods as for flowers.

directions: Sow into a sunny bed and thin so each plant can develop full, branching stems for cutting. Let the white flowers set seed to produce the signature dark, spiky pods that this strain is grown for. Cut pods when firm and well colored, then hang them upside down to dry for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or autumn for both cut flowers and pods.

directions: Direct sow into open ground and thin the seedlings to encourage strong flowering stems. White blooms give way to large, clean green pods marked with fine papillae, a favorite of cut-flower growers. Harvest the pods while still green and plump for fresh use, or leave them to dry for lasting decoration.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall where the plants are to flower.

directions: Sow the seed thinly into a sunny, well-drained spot and cover only lightly. Thin seedlings and grow in a block so the tall stems lean on one another for support. Deep violet-blue flowers carry contrasting dark centers and mature into strikingly veined maroon-tinged pods.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or autumn; a classic cut-flower selection.

directions: Direct sow into fertile, free-draining soil and thin to give each plant space for long stems. Support with netting or twigs where wind is a problem, as flowering stems are tall. Large ivory-white flowers center on a dramatic dark purple boss, followed by handsome plum-colored pods for cutting.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring or fall; each flower is uniquely marked.

directions: Sow seed shallowly into a sunny bed and thin the resulting seedlings for airflow. Grow in a group so the slender stems support each other and stay upright. White petals are brushed and streaked with blue like painted porcelain, no two flowers quite alike, and the pods dry well.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; grown mainly for its ornamental pods.

directions: Sow into a sunny, well-drained bed and thin plants to encourage tall, well-branched stems. Allow flowers to fade and set the plum-purple, balloon-like seed pods this strain is famous for. Cut the pods once fully colored and firm for fresh bouquets or dry them for long-lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or autumn; larger-flowered than common love-in-a-mist.

directions: Sow seed thinly and just cover it, keeping the ground moist until seedlings appear. Thin to give room, and grow in full sun with sharp drainage for best flowering. Broad blue flowers show off prominent red stamens and a spicy fragrance, ripening into striking horned seed pods.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall; valued for flowers and dramatic pods.

directions: Sow into loose, sunny soil and thin seedlings to prevent crowding. Keep watering moderate and avoid heavy feeding to favor blooms and firm pods. Deep blue flowers with dark red-purple stamens give way to bold, star-shaped horned pods excellent for drying.

planting.note: Sow directly in spring or fall; grown for unusual umbrella-like pods.

directions: Direct sow into a sunny bed and thin to give plants room to branch. The small yellow-green flowers are modest, so grow this one chiefly for its seed heads. When dry, the pods can be turned inside out into distinctive spidery, umbrella-shaped forms prized by flower arrangers.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or fall where the plants are to bloom.

directions: Sow seed thinly over prepared soil and barely cover, then keep evenly moist until sprouting. Thin the ferny seedlings and grow in full sun with free-draining soil. Fully double white blossoms nestle in a fine green collar, followed by the inflated striped pods typical of the species.

planting.note: Plant or divide clumps in spring once the ground can be worked.

directions: Set divisions or nursery plants at the same depth they grew before in gritty, well-drained soil. This is the toughest of the group, forming a spreading mat that returns each year in colder gardens if drainage is sharp. Shear lightly after the main flush of mauve-pink daisies to encourage a second wave of bloom.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings out after the last frost for groundcover use.

directions: Space rooted plants generously and let the low stems root as they run to knit into a drought-tolerant carpet. Once established it needs little water and thrives on slopes and bank plantings in mild-winter regions. Cut back hard after the heavy winter-to-spring bloom to keep the mat dense and tidy.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant nursery starts at their original depth in rich, well-drained soil after frost danger passes. This selection carries doubled centers that stay open even on cloudy days, unlike single-flowered types that shut without sun. Feed lightly through the season and deadhead to keep the pale silvery blooms coming from spring into summer.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Transplant into full sun and firm the soil gently around the roots. The double blooms open white with a berry-toned reverse and hold their shape through overcast weather that would close single daisies. Remove spent heads and give a balanced feed monthly for steady flowering into the warm months.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant after frost in a sunny bed or container with sharp drainage. The rich purple double flowers stay open all day regardless of light, making this a reliable performer where cloudy mornings stall other African daisies. Deadhead often and avoid soggy soil, which is the quickest way to lose plants.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Set plants in full sun in fertile, freely draining soil once nights stay mild. Yellow petals surround a deep blue central eye that reads well in mixed containers and border edges. Trim off finished flowers and keep growth compact by pinching, and enjoy the strongest show in the cooler halves of the season.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant into a sunny spot after frost and water in well to settle the roots. Blooms shift between rose and near-white with temperature, so the plant carries several shades at once. Remove spent flowers to prolong the display through spring and again as weather cools in autumn.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Transplant into full sun in soil that drains freely and never stays waterlogged. Buttery-yellow petals ring a dark eye on a mounded, heat-tolerant plant that holds up better than many in summer. Deadhead lightly and feed monthly to keep flowers arriving from late spring onward.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in a sunny, well-drained bed or pot after the last frost has passed. Warm orange petals with a dark center make a bold container filler that tolerates heat well for the genus. Keep flowers coming by removing faded heads and watering when the top inch of soil dries.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Set plants out after frost in full sun and give them room to mound. Creamy pale-yellow daisies around a bronze eye pair easily with almost any color scheme. Pinch young plants for bushiness and deadhead through the season for a long run of bloom.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun in a container or the front of a border. It flowers early and stays neat, with bright golden-yellow daisies over dark-eyed centers. Remove old flowers and keep it out of soggy soil to maintain steady bloom in spring and fall.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Transplant into a warm, sunny position with sharp drainage after frost. Golden petals surround an unusual steely-blue central disk that makes the flower stand out in plantings. Deadhead regularly and feed lightly for a strong flush during the milder stretches of the year.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in full sun in freely draining soil once the weather has settled. The petals pinch inward at the middle into a spoon shape, showing pink faces over a blue-tinged reverse. Remove spent blooms and shear back leggy stems in midseason to renew the display.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Set out plants in a sunny, sharply drained spot after frost has passed. Each white petal is crimped into a slate-blue spoon at the base, giving the flower a distinctive pinwheel look. Deadhead often and cut back straggly growth to keep the plant flowering and tidy.

planting.note: Set out plants after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in full sun after frost in soil amended for good drainage. Vivid purple daisies cover a mounded, well-branched plant that blooms freely in cooler weather. Trim off old flowers and feed monthly to sustain color through spring and into autumn.

planting.note: Start this F1 seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered and keep warm and evenly moist until it germinates in two to three weeks. This seed-raised strain is well suited to growing as an annual and flowers reliably its first year. Move hardened plants outdoors after frost and deadhead to extend the white, dark-eyed blooms.

planting.note: Start this F1 seed indoors about eight weeks before the last frost.

directions: Press seed into a warm, moist mix and expect sprouts within a few weeks. Grow the seedlings on in bright light and set them out once frost is past. The purple daisies around dark centers keep coming with regular deadheading through the season.

planting.note: Start seed indoors roughly two months before the last frost.

directions: Sow the seed just below the surface and keep it warm and lightly moist until it comes up. This blend produces a range of pink, purple, rose, and white daisies on compact, container-friendly plants. Harden off and plant out after frost, then deadhead to keep the mixed colors flowering.
